coredata.dc:title: Colorimetric and fluorescent detection of glutathione over cysteine and homocysteine with red-emitting N-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Jia, Ruonan
Jin, Kunfeng
Zhang, Jinming
Zheng, Xuejing
Wang, Shu
Zhang, Jun
coredata.dc:description: Glutathione (GSH) is an important biothiol in biological systems and serves as a significant biomarker for the health of humans. Herein, red-emitting carbon dots (p-CDs) prepared by a simple one-step solvothermal process can exclusively, rapidly and sensitively respond to GSH owing to the high content of pyridinic N. Upon addition of GSH, p-CDs instantly give a significant color change from orange to bluish-purple. The absorbance ratio of 576 nm to 486 nm is linearly proportional to the logarithmic concentration of GSH in a wide range of 12.5−800 μM, which is the level of cellular GSH. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of p-CDs quenches obviously. The LOD of p-CDs towards GSH in fluorescence mode is 0.7 μM. Moreover, p-CDs have no response to Cys, Hcy, amino acids and ions existing in biological systems. Such a promising responsiveness offers a great opportunity to accurately, sensitively and selectively detect GSH by colorimetric and fluorescent dual modes. In addition, cellulose/p-CDs hydrogels and p-CDs-based test papers have been fabricated to successfully detect GSH according to the color-change and fluorescence quenching. The biocompatible, environment-friendly and disposable cellulose/p-CDs materials readily provide a simple and visual strategy to detect GSH levels in the biomedical fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glutathione
Carbon dots
Selective detection
Colorimetric detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthetic routes of p-CDs, o-CDs and m-CDs. (b) Photographs of p-CDs, o-CDs and m-CDs aqueous solutions before and after adding GSH under visible light and 365 nm UV light.

(a) UV–vis spectra of p-CDs with different concentrations of GSH (0-800 μM). (b) Plot of the absorbance ratio of 576 nm to 486 nm (A576/A486) versus the concentration of GSH. Inset: images of the p-CDs aqueous solutions treated with a series of different concentrations of GSH under visible light (The concentration of GSH is 0, 12.5, 50, 100, 200, 400 and 800 μM from left to right). (c) Fluorescence spectra of p-CDs with different concentrations of GSH (0-800 μM). (d) Plot of the relative fluorescence intensity ((I0-I)/I0) at 616 nm versus the concentration of GSH. I0 and I represent the fluorescence intensity at 616 nm before and after the addition of GSH. Inset: images of the p-CDs aqueous solutions treated with a series of different concentrations of GSH under UV light (The concentration of GSH is 0, 12.5, 50, 100, 200, 400 and 800 μM from left to right).

(a) UV–vis spectra of p-CDs treated with GSH, Cys and Hcy. (b) Fluorescence spectra of p-CDs treated with GSH, Cys and Hcy. (c) Fluorescence intensity ratio of the peak at 616 nm of p-CDs after and before the addition of GSH, Cys, Hcy and amino acids (I/I0). (d) Fluorescence intensity ratio of the peak at 616 nm of p-CDs after and before the addition of GSH and different ions (I/I0). Excitation wavelength, 365 nm. Data represent mean ± standard deviation (SD) (n = 3).

(a) Deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectra of p-CDs, o-CDs and m-CDs for N1s. (b) 1H-NMR spectra of GSH, pyridine and the mixture of GSH and pyridine. (c) 1H-NMR spectra of the mixture of pyridine and Hcy, pyridine and Cys, pyridine and GSH.
Cellulose/CDs composite hydrogels and CDs-based test papers for the colorimetric and fluorescent detection of GSH.
Detection range and LOD for GSH on various methods and sensors.

row.entry: MnO2 nanosheets on boron nitride quantum dots

head.ce:title: Colorimetric and fluorescent detection of glutathione over cysteine and homocysteine with red-emitting N-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Chengdu University of Technology, Chengdu 610059, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials and Chemistry & Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials and Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Chengdu University of Technology, Chengdu 610059, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Zhongguancun North First Street 2, 100190 Beijing, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red carbon dots with a high content of pyridinic N exhibited a visual, rapid, selective and accurate detection of glutathione according to the color-change and fluorescence-off.

Glutathione (GSH) is an important biothiol in biological systems and serves as a significant biomarker for the health of humans. Herein, red-emitting carbon dots (p-CDs) prepared by a simple one-step solvothermal process can exclusively, rapidly and sensitively respond to GSH owing to the high content of pyridinic N. Upon addition of GSH, p-CDs instantly give a significant color change from orange to bluish-purple. The absorbance ratio of 576 nm to 486 nm is linearly proportional to the logarithmic concentration of GSH in a wide range of 12.5−800 μM, which is the level of cellular GSH. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of p-CDs quenches obviously. The LOD of p-CDs towards GSH in fluorescence mode is 0.7 μM. Moreover, p-CDs have no response to Cys, Hcy, amino acids and ions existing in biological systems. Such a promising responsiveness offers a great opportunity to accurately, sensitively and selectively detect GSH by colorimetric and fluorescent dual modes. In addition, cellulose/p-CDs hydrogels and p-CDs-based test papers have been fabricated to successfully detect GSH according to the color-change and fluorescence quenching. The biocompatible, environment-friendly and disposable cellulose/p-CDs materials readily provide a simple and visual strategy to detect GSH levels in the biomedical fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH) plays a vital role in biological systems, including maintaining immune system function and providing a reduced environment in the cells [1–4]. It prevents oxidative damage triggered by free radicals and reactive oxygen species, and manipulates intracellular redox homeostasis, and hence, serves as a significant biomarker for the health of humans. The abnormal level of GSH is closely related to many diseases, such as cancer, neurodegenerative diseases, retarded growth rate, liver damage, and cardiovascular diseases. Thus, the detection of GSH is of great importance and has been widely investigated. So far, many strategies, such as liquid chromatography, electrochemical devices, Raman spectroscopy and optical sensors have been developed and used to detect GSH [5–16]. Among them, the optical sensors have attracted increasing attention, owing to their high sensitivity, simple operation and short analysis time. However, for the optical sensors, the detection of GSH mainly relies on the processes of recognizing the strongly nucleophilic thiol group, such as Michael addition reaction, disulfide cleavage reaction and sulfonamide cleavage reaction [17–28]. Other biothiols in cells, such as cysteine (Cys) and homocysteine (Hcy), have a pronounced influence on the recognition process of GSH. Therefore, it is essential and difficult to fabricate a GSH-specific sensor.

Recently, Yin et al. developed two novel multi-signal fluorescent probes with four potential reaction sites for simultaneous and rapid sensing of Cys, Hcy, and GSH from three emission channels based on different reaction mechanisms between probe and biothiols [29–31]. In addition, Xu et al. developed a dual-channel turn-on fluorescent probe, comprising naphthalimide and cyanine bridged by a thiol-reactive sulfonamide moiety [32]. The probe selectively responded to GSH in visible and near-infrared channels synchronously and independently. Niu et al. fabricated BODIPY-based sensors based on the different reactivity of GSH, Cys and Hcy [33,34]. The significantly different photophysical properties of sulfur- and amino-substituted BODIPY enabled the discrimination of GSH over Cys and Hcy. But, these GSH-responsive sensors generally have sophisticated molecular structures, and their synthesis processes are time consuming. Moreover, the obtained optical sensors usually suffer from poor water solubility and high toxicity which severely limit their practical applications. Hence, it is still intriguing to fabricate an optical sensor with simple synthetic route, low cost, low toxicity and good solubility for quick-responsive, sensitive and high-selective detection of GSH.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a kind of luminescent carbon-nanomaterial which can be easily synthesized at low cost [35]. They have received tremendous attention in the fields of sensing, bio-imaging, biomedicine and optoelectronic devices [36–38], because of their superior properties including low toxicity, good photostability, excellent biocompatibility, cell membrane permeability, water-solubility and tunable surface functionalities [39,40].

In this work, we employed phenylenediamines as the carbon sources to prepare three CDs by a simple one-step solvothermal process. The obtained red-emitting CDs (p-CDs) selectively, visually and accurately detect GSH over Cys, Hcy, amino acids and ions by colorimetric and fluorescent dual modes. The responsive mechanism has been discussed. Moreover, p-CDs-based hydrogels and test papers have been readily fabricated to successfully detect GSH in solid state according to the color-change and fluorescence-off.

A quantity of 1.296 g p-PD (o-PD or m-PD) was dissolved in 120 mL N,N′-dimethylformamide (DMF), and then the solution was transferred into poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclaves. After heating at 200 °C for 5 h, dark-red or green grey suspensions were obtained. The crude products were purified with silica column chromatography using the mixture of dichloromethane and methanol as eluents. After removing the solvents and further drying under vacuum, the purified p-CDs, o-CDs and m-CDs were obtained. The obtained p-CDs, o-CDs and m-CDs powders were dissolved in water or methanol for use.

Cotton pulp of 0.25 g was added into 1-allyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride (AmimCl) of 10 g, then the mixture was stirred vigorously at 80 °C to get a homogeneous solution. Next, the solution was kept in the vacuum oven for 2 h to remove air bubbles. Then, the solution was slowly poured into 12-well plate, and was subsequently put into the coagulation bath (AmimCl/H2O, v/v = 1/1) for 1 day. Then, the coagulation bath was changed to ethanol/H2O (v/v = 1/1) to completely remove the ionic liquid AmimCl. Finally, the obtained cellulose hydrogel was immersed in CDs aqueous solutions for 1 day to get the cellulose/CDs composite hydrogels.
The CDs aqueous solutions were brushed onto the filter papers, which were treated by DMF to remove the luminogens. After drying, CDs-based test papers were obtained.

Hela cells were cultured in dulbecco’s modified eagle medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10 % FBS at 37 °C in a humid atmosphere containing 5% CO2. Hela cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 104 cells/well, and the cells were adhered to the bottom of the plate after 12–24 h. Then, p-CDs with a series of concentrations were added (100 μL/well). After incubation in the dark for 4–24 h, the supernatant was removed and the Hela cells were cultured in DMEM for 24 h. Then, MTT (0.5 mg/mL in DMEM, 100 μL/well) was added. After incubation at 37 °C for 4 h, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) (100 μL/well) was added to sufficiently dissolve the produced formazan by live cells. After shaking the plate for 5 min, the absorbance at 520 nm was recorded with a microplate reader. All the samples were performed in triplicate. The cell viability was calculated according to the equation:
v i a b i l i t y
%
=
A
A 0
× 100
Where A is the absorbance at 520 nm of experimental groups, and A0 is the absorbance at 520 nm of control groups.

Three CDs (p-CDs, o-CDs and m-CDs) were prepared from phenylenediamine and its isomers by a simple one-step solvothermal process [41], as shown in Fig. 1 . The obtained p-CDs, o-CDs and m-CDs exhibit different visible and fluorescence colors, in which p-CDs have orange visible color and red-emitting fluorescence, o-CDs have yellow visible and fluorescence colors, and m-CDs have khaki visible color and blue-emitting fluorescence. After addition of GSH, the orange visible color of p-CDs instantly changes to bluish-purple, which is quite easily observed by naked eye. The red-emitting fluorescence of p-CDs quenches at the meantime. For the o-CDs, the addition of GSH deepens the yellow visible color, and makes the yellow-emitting fluorescence quenching. For the m-CDs, the addition of GSH has no impact on the visible and fluorescence colors. Thus, we choose p-CDs as a sensor to rapidly and visually detect GSH by colorimetric and fluorescent dual modes, which is more efficient than the single mode detection.

The responsiveness of p-CDs to GSH was investigated by colorimetric and fluorescent dual modes (Fig. 2 ). p-CDs have a maximum absorbance peak at 486 nm, as shown in Fig. 2a. After addition of GSH, the peak intensity of 486 nm decreases, and a new absorbance peak at 576 nm appears. The peak intensity of 576 nm increases gradually as the concentration of GSH increases. During the concentration of GSH in a range of 12.5−800 μM, the absorbance ratio of 576 nm to 486 nm (A576/A486) is linearly proportional to the logarithmic concentration of GSH (Fig. 2b). Thus, we can get a calibration equation between the A576/A486 and the logarithmic concentration of GSH. Based on the calibration equation, the GSH concentration can be quantitatively detected. Furthermore, the visible color of p-CDs aqueous solutions simultaneously changed from orange to purple and bluish-purple as the concentration of GSH increases. So, we can visually determine the concentration range of GSH according to the different colors. This colorimetric strategy is rapid, convenient and easy-to-operate without complicated instruments. The fluorescence spectra of p-CDs are shown in Fig. 2c. It is obvious that the fluorescence of p-CDs gradually quenches as the concentration of GSH increases. The fluorescence intensity at 616 nm (I 616) sharply decreases during the concentration of GSH in a range of 0−100 μM (Fig. 2d). The limit of detection (LOD) for GSH is 0.7 μM. Previous methods and materials used to detect GSH are summarized in Table 1 . It can be seen that the LOD and detection range of p-CDs are comparable to those of some materials. Besides, the physiological concentration of GSH is millimolar range in cells and 1−2 μM in plasma and extracellular fluids [42,43]. The GSH concentration in cells exactly fits the detection range of p-CDs in visible mode and is much higher than the LOD of p-CDs in fluorescence mode. In addition, because m-CDs with blue emission are insensitive to GSH, the ratiometric fluorescent sensor was prepared by using p-CDs as the indicator and m-CDs as the reference (Fig. S1). As the GSH concentration increase, the emission at 450 nm is almost unchanged, while the emission at 616 nm decreases. The fluorescent color changes from red to blue with the increase of the GSH concentration. Thus, the p-CDs can be readily used as colorimetric and fluorescent sensor for rapid, visual and sensitive detection of GSH in practice. p-CDs not only can quantitatively and qualitatively detect GSH by UV–vis absorbance and fluorescence spectra, but also can visually monitor GSH depending on the different colors.

It is well-known that the selective responsiveness is of significant importance to a sensor. In the biological systems, there are many kinds of components, such as amino acids, ions, GSH, Cys and Hcy [44]. Especially, Cys and Hcy have similar structures and functional groups with GSH, so they usually make an impact on the monitoring of GSH. The responsiveness of p-CDs to amino acids, ions, Cys and Hcy, was also investigated, as shown in Fig. 3 . It can be seen that, after the addition of Cys and Hcy, the UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of p-CDs have a negligible change. While after adding GSH, a new absorbance peak at 576 nm appears and the fluorescence of p-CDs quenches obviously. These phenomena mean that p-CDs could selectively detect GSH among biothiols. Besides, we tested whether or not the amino acids and ions existing in biological systems could be responded by p-CDs. From Fig. 3c-d, it is clear that the fluorescence of p-CDs is unchanged after adding amino acids and ions. While upon addition of GSH, the fluorescence intensity of p-CDs reduces more than 90 %. In other words, the GSH detection with p-CDs cannot be interfered by other biomolecules and ions. The p-CDs were successfully used to detect practical samples without and with the addition of GSH, such as blood plasma and urine (Fig. S2 and S3). Therefore, these above phenomena demonstrate that p-CDs have an extremely high selectivity to GSH.

To investigate the different responsiveness of p-CDs, o-CDs and m-CDs to GSH, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of p-CDs, o-CDs and m-CDs were measured (Fig. S4). All three CDs are composed of the same elements, including C, N and O. But the relative contents of C, N and O elements in p-CDs, o-CDs and m-CDs are different, which is maybe the reason that p-CDs, o-CDs and m-CDs have different fluorescence characteristics [41]. Furthermore, deconvoluted high resolution XPS spectra were carried out to illustrate the chemical bonds in p-CDs (Fig. S5). As shown in Fig. 4 a, the deconvoluted N1s signals of p-CDs, o-CDs and m-CDs are quite different. It can be seen that p-CDs have the highest content of pyridinic N among all three CDs, while there is almost none of pyridinic N in m-CDs, which have no response to GSH.

To determine whether or not pyridinic N is the reason that p-CDs, o-CDs and m-CDs exhibit different responsiveness to GSH, we recorded the 1H-NMR spectra of GSH, pyridine and the mixture of GSH and pyridine. 1H-NMR signals of GSH and pyridine are obviously split (Fig. 4b). Upon addition of pyridine into GSH, the spin-spin splitting of all peaks in both GSH and pyridine disappears, and all peaks become broad. At the meantime, the 2-H peak in GSH shifts from 4.0 ppm to 3.8 ppm. These results demonstrate that GSH forms strong interactions with pyridine. From 1H-NMR spectra of pyridine/Hcy and pyridine/Cys, the change of all signals is inconspicuous (Fig. 4c). 1H-NMR spectra of p-CDs exhibit the similar phenomena after adding GSH, Cys or Hcy (Fig. S6). The addition of GSH makes the peaks of p-CDs in a range of 6.5–8.5 ppm give a remarkable shift, while the addition of Cys and Hcy results in a slight change. In addition, after adding pyrrole or aniline in GSH solutions, the peaks of GSH, pyrrole or aniline have a negligible change (Fig. S7). Thus, the high content of pyridinic N in p-CDs is responsible for the excellent responsiveness of p-CDs to GSH.

Considering the condition for practical applications, the biocompatibility of p-CDs towards mammalian cells was tested (Fig. S8). It can be seen that p-CDs exhibit negligible cytotoxicity on Hela cells in the concentration range of 5−40 μg/mL, indicating the good safety and biocompatibility of p-CDs. In addition, cellulose is the most abundant biopolymer on earth [45]. By simply blending and coating processes, cellulose/CDs composite hydrogels and CDs-based test papers were fabricated (Fig. 5 ). The composite hydrogels and CDs-based test papers possess the good biocompatibility and biodegradability, because they consist of cellulose and CDs. As shown in Fig. 5, cellulose/p-CDs hydrogels and p-CDs-based test papers give an obvious color change and fluorescence-off upon addition of GSH. Cellulose/m-CDs hydrogels and m-CDs-based test papers have no response to GSH, which is similar with the GSH-responsive behavior of m-CDs aqueous solutions. Surprisingly, cellulose/o-CDs hydrogels and o-CDs-based test papers exhibit more obvious GSH-responsiveness than o-CDs aqueous solutions, perhaps because of the amplification effect of the high content of CDs in hydrogels and test papers. Meanwhile, the p-CDs-based test papers selectively and visually respond to GSH over Cys and Hcy (Fig. S9). The LOD for GSH is 1 mM by the naked eyes. Therefore, cellulose/CDs hydrogels and CDs-based test papers from p-CDs and o-CDs can visually monitor GSH.

Compared with CDs aqueous solutions, the CDs-based hydrogels and test papers are easier to use and operate, thus they exhibit a broader application prospect. For example, cellulose/p-CDs hydrogel and test paper can be readily applied to selectively detect GSH in physiological fluids. Their colorimetric and fluorescent dual modes detection will greatly simplify the detection process [46]. More importantly, both cellulose/CDs hydrogels and CDs-based test papers are environment-friendly and disposable.

Three phenylenediamine-based carbon dots (p-CDs, o-CDs and m-CDs) were prepared by a simple one-step solvothermal process. Among them, red-emitting p-CDs instantly exhibit a significant color change from orange to bluish-purple and a fluorescence quenching, upon the addition of GSH. The absorbance ratio of 576 nm to 486 nm of p-CDs is linearly proportional to the GSH concentration. The LOD in fluorescence mode is 0.7 μM, which is much lower than the level of cellular GSH. More importantly, p-CDs have no response to other biothiols, amino acids and ions existing in biological systems. Thus, p-CDs not only can quantitatively detect GSH by UV–vis absorbance and fluorescence spectra, but also can visually monitor GSH basing on the different colors. Furthermore, via physical blending and coating processes, cellulose/p-CDs composite hydrogels and p-CDs-based test strips with good biocompatibility, biodegradability and disposability have been readily fabricated to give a colorimetric and fluorescent detection of GSH.

Ruonan Jia: Conceptualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Kunfeng Jin: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jinming Zhang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Validation, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Xuejing Zheng: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Shu Wang: Supervision, Resources, Writing - review & editing. Jun Zhang: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2017YFA0403103), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51425307, 51573196 and 51773210) and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association CAS (No. 2018040).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128506.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Glutathione system in animal model of solid tumors: from regulation to therapeutic target

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.R. Corso, A. Acco, Glutathione system in animal model of solid tumors: From regulation to therapeutic target, Crit. Rev. Oncol. Hemat. 128 (2018) 43-57.
A. Meister, Metabolism and functions of glutathione, Trends Biochem. Sci. 6 (1981) 231-234.
N.S. Kosower, E.M. Kosower, The glutathione status of cells, Int. Rev. Cytol. 54 (1978) 109-160.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The importance of glutathione in human disease

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.M. Townsend, K.D. Tew, H. Tapiero, The importance of glutathione in human disease, Biomed. Pharmacother. 57 (2003) 145-155.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of iron phthalocyanine functionalized N,B-doped reduced graphene oxide nanocomposites and sensitive electrochemical detection for glutathione

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Lei, Y. Zhou, R.Q. Zhu, Y. Liu, C. Dong, S.M. Shuang, Facile synthesis of iron phthalocyanine functionalized N,B-doped reduced graphene oxide nanocomposites and sensitive electrochemical detection for glutathione, Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem. 297 (2019) 126756.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence biosensor for glutathione detection based on poly-L-lysine as co-reactant and graphene-based poly(luminol/aniline) as nanoprobes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.X. Wang, L.M. Chen, P.J. Wang, M.S. Li, D.F. Liu, A novel ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence biosensor for glutathione detection based on poly-L-lysine as co-reactant and graphene-based poly(luminol/aniline) as nanoprobes, Biosens. Bioelectron. 133 (2019) 154-159.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Peptide-induced aggregation of glutathione-capped gold nanoclusters: a new strategy for designing aggregation-induced enhanced emission probes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.G. You, W.L. Tseng, Peptide-induced aggregation of glutathione-capped gold nanoclusters: A new strategy for designing aggregation-induced enhanced emission probes, Anal. Chim. Acta 1078 (2019) 101-111.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Glutathione regulated inner filter effect of MnO2 nanosheets on boron nitride quantum dots for sensitive assay

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Peng, H.H. Xing, X.S. Fan, Y. Xue, J. Li, E. Wang, Glutathione regulated inner filter effect of MnO2 nanosheets on boron nitride quantum dots for sensitive assay, Anal. Chem. 91 (2019) 5762-5767.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Light-responsive metal-organic framework as an oxidase mimic for cellular glutathione detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.F. Liu, M. Zhou, W. Cao, X.Y. Wang, Q. Wang, S.R. Sirong Li, H. Wei, Light-responsive metal-organic framework as an oxidase mimic for cellular glutathione detection, Anal. Chem. 91 (2019) 8170-8175.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-colored carbon dot encapsulated metal-organic framework for ratiometric detection of glutathione

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Jalili, A. Khataee, M.R. Rashidi, R. Luque, Dual-colored carbon dot encapsulated metal-organic framework for ratiometric detection of glutathione, Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem. 297 (2019) 126775.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-performance liquid chromatography of thiols and disulfides: Dinitrophenol derivatives

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.W. Fariss, D.J. Reed, High-performance liquid chromatography of thiols and disulfides: Dinitrophenol derivatives, Method. Enzymol. 143 (1987) 101-109.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical determination of glutathione: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.C. Harfield, C. Batchelor-McAuley, R. G. Compton, Electrochemical determination of glutathione: A review, Analyst 137 (2012) 2285-2296.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Real-time monitoring of glutathione-triggered thiopurine anticancer drug release in live cells investigated by surface-enhanced Raman scattering

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Ock, W.I. Jeon, E.O. Ganbold, M. Kim, J. Park, J.H. Seo, K. Cho, S.W. Joo, S.Y. Lee, Real-time monitoring of glutathione-triggered thiopurine anticancer drug release in live cells investigated by surface-enhanced Raman scattering, Anal. Chem. 84 (2012) 2172-2178.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A turn-on fluorescent sensor for the discrimination of cystein from homocystein and glutathione

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.Y. Niu, Y.S. Guan, Y.Z. Chen, L.Z. Wu, C.H. Tung, Q.Z. Yang, A turn-on fluorescent sensor for the discrimination of cystein from homocystein and glutathione, Chem. Commun. 49 (2013) 1294-1296.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A coumarin-based “off-on” sensor for fluorescence selectivily discriminating GSH from Cys/Hcy and its bioimaging in living cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.Q. Li, F.J. Huo, Y.K. Yue, Y.B. Zhang, C.X. Yin, A coumarin-based “off-on” sensor for fluorescence selectivily discriminating GSH from Cys/Hcy and its bioimaging in living cells, Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem. 253 (2017) 42-49.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rhodamine 6G conjugated-quantum dots used for highly sensitive and selective ratiometric fluorescence sensor of glutathione

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.J. Gui, X.Q. An, H.J. Su, W. G. Shen, L.Y. Zhu, X.Y. Ma, Z.Y. Chen, X.Y. Wang, Rhodamine 6G conjugated-quantum dots used for highly sensitive and selective ratiometric fluorescence sensor of glutathione, Talanta 94 (2012) 295-300.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitroolefin-based coumarin as a colorimetric and fluorescent dual probe for biothiols

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.Q. Sun, M.L. Chen, J. Liu, X. Lv, J.F. Li, W. Guo, Nitroolefin-based coumarin as a colorimetric and fluorescent dual probe for biothiols, Chem. Commun. 47 (2011) 11029-11031.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Disulfide-cleavage-triggered chemosensors and their biological applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.H. Lee, Z.G. Yang, C.W. Lim, Y.H. Lee, S. Dongbang, C. Kang, J.S. Kim, Disulfide-cleavage-triggered chemosensors and their biological applications, Chem. Rev. 113 (2013) 5071-5109.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis and characterization of a series of highly fluorogenic substrates for glutathione transferases, a general strategy

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ruonan Jia obtained her BSc in Polymer Chemistry and Physics at the Beijing University of Chemical Technology in 2014. Then, under the supervision of Dr Jun Zhang and Dr Shu Wang, she is investigating the design, fabrication and biomedical application of novel cellulose-based materials at the Key Laboratory of Engineering Plastics, Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences (ICCAS). In 2019, She received her PhD at ICCAS. Presently, she joined the College of Materials and Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Chengdu University of Technology. Her research focuses on fluorescent sensors and functional biomass materials.

Jinming Zhang is an associate professor of polymer science at the Key Laboratory of Engineering Plastics, Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences (ICCAS). He received his PhD at ICCAS in 2010. His research focuses on functional materials based on natural polysaccharides, new polysaccharide derivatives, and a novel method to prepare polysaccharide-based materials in ionic liquids.
